does: This paper demonstrates that machine unlearning methods certified at bfloat16 precision become vulnerable to quantization-induced recovery attacks when deployed at INT4 precision, where forgotten information is systematically restored. The authors conduct the first systematic evaluation of unlearning robustness under INT4 quantization in the NF4+LoRA setting, testing seven unlearning methods on LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct across three datasets (TOFU, MUSE-News, WikiBio-WPU). They document recovery rates up to 22x, showing the vulnerability worsens with dataset difficulty and that INT8 quantization does not exhibit this problem. The work identifies the FA-RA-Q-INT4 trilemma, where no existing method simultaneously achieves strong forgetting, high utility, and quantization robustness, revealing a sharp phase transition in the accuracy-robustness tradeoff. They propose DURABLEUN-SAF, a sharpness-aware forgetting approach designed to address this vulnerability.
connects: This work extends recent research on machine unlearning robustness and privacy-preserving machine learning, building on methods like LoRA-based unlearning and existing forget-retain-erase frameworks. It connects to literature on model quantization and adversarial robustness, recognizing that precision reduction can expose forgotten information similar to how adversarial perturbations can recover private data. The paper responds to gaps in compliance auditing practices that assume deployment conditions match evaluation conditions, mirroring concerns in adversarial robustness about distribution shift between training-time verification and deployment-time threats. It builds on prior work studying the malleability of neural networks under different precisions and extends unlearning evaluation beyond binary success metrics to consider practical deployment scenarios where models are systematically compressed.
enables: This research enables more rigorous unlearning compliance auditing that accounts for real-world deployment conditions, particularly the ubiquitous practice of INT4 quantization in production LLMs. It opens investigation into the fundamental tradeoffs between privacy, utility, and computational efficiency in machine unlearning, suggesting that achieving all three simultaneously may require novel architectural approaches. The work points toward future defenses that specifically target quantization-induced vulnerabilities, motivating research into sharpness-aware training objectives and other robustness mechanisms. It could inspire broader examination of how other model compression techniques (pruning, distillation, sparsification) interact with unlearning guarantees. The sharp phase transition discovery invites theoretical work explaining why quantization recovery occurs and when robustness becomes achievable, potentially leading to certified unlearning methods that remain robust across precision levels.
